title: Substrate insulated Josephson junctions for superconducting quantum circuits
authors: U. Strobel, L. Radtke, L. Kamps, J. N. Voss, J. Lisenfeld, J. Luo-Hofmann, D. Reuter, S. Masis, A. V. Ustinov, H. Rotzinger
abstract: We have developed a fabrication technique for Josephson junctions that employs a three-dimensional patterned, low-loss substrate instead of commonly used organic resists. The technique enables the fabrication of high-quality trilayer junctions from a wide range of geometries and materials, including high-melting-point superconductors such as tantalum or niobium. The junction electrodes are free from intentionally introduced oxides and organic materials, which are known sources of decoherence. We fabricate and characterize underdamped Nb/AlOx/Nb junctions of different sizes in several geometries. Such junctions enable manufacturing of quantum circuits operating at higher speeds and elevated temperatures.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough fabrication technique for Josephson junctions by utilizing substrate profiling instead of traditional organic resists. This method yields high-quality, oxide-free junctions suitable for advanced superconducting quantum circuits. The results demonstrate improved junction performance and scalability across various complex geometries.
main_problem: The primary challenge is developing fabrication techniques for Josephson junctions (JJs) that avoid intentionally introduced oxides or organic residues, which are major sources of decoherence in superconducting quantum circuits.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated substrate profiling methods to create high-quality, oxide-free JJs using various geometries (SEI, MT, cT), enabling operation at higher speeds and temperatures.
method: The core method involves etching patterned trenches into the substrate material itself to define the junction barrier and electrodes, circumventing traditional resist-based lithography steps.
model_or_system: The platform is superconducting quantum circuits utilizing Josephson junctions (e.g., Nb/AlOx/Nb). The geometry is defined by creating specific step profiles on the substrate rather than relying on deposited insulating layers or organic resists.
key_observables: I-V characterization curves showing pronounced hysteresis, extracted gap voltage ($2.78 	ext{ mV}$), critical current density ($J_c$), and microwave resonator quality factor ($Q > 3 	imes 10^5$).
important_parameters: Operating temperatures (e.g., $4.2 	ext{ K}$), junction sizes, and the ratio of edge leakage to normal resistance ($R_{sg}/R_n$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often requires correcting simple approximations for small junctions using complex fitting procedures involving effective temperature ($\Theta_{	ext{eff}}$) or deposition parameters ($p \cdot t$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate cross-sections and I-V curves for various junction types (SEI, cSEI, MT), alongside plots showing $J_c$ dependence on processing time/pressure and resonator performance.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by introducing the limitations of current JJ fabrication, detailing several novel substrate profiling geometries (SEI, MT, etc.), presenting characterization results via I-V curves and microwave measurements, and finally analyzing the derived junction parameters ($J_c$, leakage ratios) across different structures.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses hardware limitations in superconducting qubits by improving junction quality and scalability through novel material engineering techniques, which is central to quantum computing architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Step edge insulated tunnel junction. (a) Substrate cross-section after forming the step with an undercut. (b) Trilayer deposition. To ensure M2 continuity across the step, we require δ > α + β −ϵ. (c) The top electrode (TE) is formed by lithography. (d) The bottom electrode (BE) is formed by lithography. The current direction is shown by the black arrow. (e) False-color optical image of an Nb/AlOx/Nb SEI junction. The junction area is enclosed by the dashed-line rectangle. (f) A typical I-V characterization of a junction with nominal area A = 100 µm2 at 4.2 K indicates an underdamped junction with a linear dissipative branch for bias current above Ic = 110 µA. The return path shows...

FIG. 2. (a) cSEI (steps I-II-IIIa-IVa) and p-cSEI (steps I-II-IIIb-IVb-Vb) junctions. (b) Cross-sectional scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of a trench, taken at 55◦tilt after a 4 nm Au sputter and a focused ion beam (FIB) cut. The 800 nm wide trench is etched into a silicon substrate using the Bosch undercut process. The undercut dimensions are in good agreement with the design parameters of α = 450 nm, β = 250 nm and γ = 100 nm. (c) p-cSEI Nb/AlOx/Nb/Nb junction cross-sectional SEM image.

FIG. 4. (a) I–V characteristic of an Nb cT junction with A = 16µm2 at 50 mK. Inset I. Sample state after (1) no- undercut trench etch (γ ≤0), (2) M1 deposition (ϵ > α + β), and (3) planarization. BE is formed in the trench. Inset II Sample state after (4) hydrofluric acid dip and ion-mill cleaning to remove the native oxide, (5) TB1 and M2 depo- sition, (6) TE patterning, and (7) TB1 patterning (optional, possibly with the same mask as the previous step).(b) False- color SEM image of Nb cT junction(c) Cross-sectional SEM image
